ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'honey drink' class; no specific entry for packaged flavoured honey drink. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers honey-based beverages broadly; no specific code for packaged flavoured variant. Closest parent code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged flavoured honey drink; null used to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one 250 ml bottle, equivalent to approximately 250 g, providing about 103 kcal and 24.3 g of carbohydrates predominantly from honey-derived sugars.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured honey drink (packaged) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Flavoured honey drink (packaged)', cr_id D05010016, with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value 58 — International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008); corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged flavoured honey drink. GI of 58 is derived from honey (pure) as the primary carbohydrate source, per Atkinson FS et al., 'International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, and the University of Sydney GI Database entry for honey. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (58 × 24.3 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 14.1, rounded to 15.0 to account for dilution and added flavourings. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may vary by formulation.

hypertension.key_factor: High free-sugar content (9.7 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, despite low sodium (21 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this drink; the 9.7 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and contributes to BP elevation through fructose-driven uric acid production and endothelial dysfunction, independent of its sodium content (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.7 g sugar/100 g and GI ~58; honey's fructose-glucose mix causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation in liquid form with no fibre to attenuate absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this drink entirely; it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering rapidly absorbed free sugars (9.7 g/100 g, predominantly honey-derived fructose and glucose) with zero dietary fibre, causing acute postprandial hyperglycaemia and is not made safer by the 'natural' honey label.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation, visceral adiposity, and endothelial inflammation; this drink provides 9.7 g free sugar/100 g with no protective nutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this drink; its high free-sugar content (9.7 g/100 g) with no fibre, omega-3, or antioxidant benefit contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and systemic inflammation, both established CVD risk factors per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar intake (9.7 g/100 g) with no fibre or anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this drink; its high free-sugar content and absence of any anti-inflammatory factor (no omega-3, polyphenols, fibre >3 g, or anti-inflammatory spices) make it pro-inflammatory and counterproductive to RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Honey Drink (Packaged) is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened beverage sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, typically in bottles or cartons of 250–500 ml. It is formulated with water, honey, added food-grade flavourings, and often additional sugar or sweeteners to enhance palatability. The drink has a light golden colour, a mild floral-sweet taste characteristic of diluted honey, and a thin, watery texture with no viscosity. It is consumed chilled as a refreshment and is commonly found in convenience stores, supermarkets, and vending machines across Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides 41 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates (10.41 g/100 g) as the dominant macronutrient, of which 9.7 g/100 g is free sugar derived primarily from honey. Protein (0.08 g/100 g) and fat (0.0 g/100 g) are negligible. Dietary fibre is absent (0.0 g/100 g), and sodium is low at 21 mg/100 g. Despite the 'natural honey' positioning, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and carries the same metabolic risks as other SSBs: rapid postprandial glucose elevation, insulin resistance promotion, and cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 58 (medium), based on honey as the primary carbohydrate source. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 103 kcal and 24.3 g of carbohydrates, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 15. It is vegetarian and gluten-free but not vegan due to honey content.
common_user_queries: Is packaged honey drink good for diabetics?
Is honey drink suitable for high blood pressure?
Is honey drink better than soft drinks?
Can I drink honey drink if I have diabetes?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010016) with a reported confidence of 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using honey as the primary carbohydrate source, which is clearly documented.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero by the HPB source for this formulation — this is a water-based beverage with no fat-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as this is a filtered liquid honey drink with no whole-food fibre sources. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged honey drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D05010016) as of 2026-04-16. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source and are confirmed zero. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on honey (pure) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for packaged flavoured honey drink was found in approved sources. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are preserved as contextually accurate (sodium 21 mg/100 g; energy 41 kcal/100 g). Vegan flag set to false as honey is an animal-derived product.
